evidence_quotes.quote: revoking access to games their customers already bought as goods.

paragraphs.text: Hello. An additional protection that I think the Digital Fairness Act should have is for it to provide consumer protections against the practice of video game publishers revoking access to games their customers already bought as goods. This is typically done by making such games entirely depend on publisher-ran servers online, if a publisher decides to shutdown said servers, the game customers paid for can no longer be played. This is planned obsolescence that is anti-consumer and against the preservation of media that future generations can experience and learn from first-hand. In many countries there is no precedent on the legality of revoking access to games customers purchased. I believe that the Digital Fairness Act should implement protections against the destruction of video games by mandating video game publishers to create a robust end-of-life plan for their future games, so that they are left in a reasonably playable state after official support ends. Such end-of-life plans can include releasing a software package to customers which allows them to run the servers the game depends on, or at least releasing tools that customers can use to create a emulation of the servers. The StopKillingGames movement created a now closed Europeans Citizens Initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames" which gathered over 1,000,000 signatures, which shows that there is a sizeable population of EU citizens who want protections against video game destruction. A video game called "The Crew" had its servers shut down without any way for customers to continue playing the game. StopKillingGames are encouraging French owners of "The Crew" to contact the UFC-Que Choisir about the revocation of their access. There is growing support for such protections outside the EU as well: A now closed UK Government partition called "Prohibit publishers irrevocably disabling video games they have already sold" got over 150,000 signatures and UK Parliament will debate the partition on November 3rd 2025. With how large of a market the EU is to the video game industry, protections against video game destruction can force publishers to adhere if they want to keep their EU customer base. And also potentially apply such protections outside of the EU to reduce the overhead of maintaining differing regional polices. The EU has the potential to set the path to a more consumer-friendly video game industry both within the EU and globally. Please consider my feedback and consider adding protections against video game destruction to the Digital Fairness Act. Thank You.
